measured_on: private 728-file Next.js App Router monorepo (var.gg apps/frontend) — not shareable
note: A quick A/B of tsc vs tsgo on one real repo, not a rigorous timing distribution. Timings are self-reported via PowerShell Measure-Command over a handful of runs (cold = after deleting tsconfig.tsbuildinfo; warm = incremental cache present). The durable, portable claims are (1) tsgo is meaningfully faster but the '10x' headline is scale-dependent (~3x at 728 files), and (2) tsc and tsgo-preview diagnostics can DIVERGE. Absolute seconds and diagnostic counts are codebase-specific and are NOT third-party reproducible; only the method (run.sh) is. No raw sample arrays are kept — summary only.

metrics.label: tsc --noEmit vs tsgo --noEmit on the same repo
metrics.measured_on: private 728-file Next.js App Router monorepo (var.gg apps/frontend)
metrics.ratio_desc: ~3.2x faster cold (6.7s -> 2.1s), ~2.7x faster warm (1.9s -> 0.7s) on our repo.
metrics.caveat: The official '10x' headline is for LARGE codebases. At our scale (728 files, skipLibCheck on) the speedup is ~3x, NOT 10x — honest and reproducible only in trend, not in absolute value. Absolute seconds were measured on a private monorepo and are not third-party reproducible. run.sh reproduces the METHOD on any repo; the durable claim is 'meaningfully faster, magnitude scale-dependent', not a fixed multiple.

findings.claim: tsc 6.0.3 and tsgo 7.0-dev do NOT report bit-identical diagnostics on the same source.
findings.detail: tsc found 3 errors, all stale generated-file references (TS2307 in .next/types/**, a dangling nf404probe route type — not source bugs). tsgo found 4: the same 3 PLUS one extra TS2769 'No overload matches this call' at src/features/abbreviations/hooks/useInfiniteAbbreviations.ts(82,9), in a useInfiniteQuery({ ... initialData ... }) overload. The file carries //@ts-ignore at lines 67/71/76, but the TS2769 is at line 82 on initialData, so it is a genuinely separate diagnostic, not a suppressed one.
findings.interpretation: tsc 6.0.3 and tsgo 7.0-dev are DIFFERENT type-checker versions, so a divergence is not surprising. Whether this specific one is (a) an intended 7.0 tightening, (b) a preview bug, or (c) a 6.0 -> 7.0 overload-selection difference is NOT determinable from this run alone.
findings.lesson: Preview diagnostics are not yet guaranteed 100% tsc-identical. When migrating, always DIFF the diagnostics between tsc and tsgo — do not assume a green tsc means a green tsgo, or that tsgo's extra error is a real source bug.

explicitly_not_verified.note: The CAUSE of the diagnostic divergence was not determined. Attributing it to intended tightening vs preview bug vs overload-selection change would require bisecting compiler versions and/or filing an upstream repro — out of scope for this quick A/B.

The official '10x' claim was NOT reproduced and NOT tested at the scale it targets. We measured only our 728-file repo (~3x). We did not run a large (e.g. 100k+ file) codebase where the 10x figure is claimed to hold, so we neither confirm nor refute it there.

caveats.note: Absolute seconds AND diagnostic counts are specific to var.gg's private 728-file monorepo. A third party cannot reproduce these exact numbers — they do not have our source. Only the METHOD (run.sh) is reproducible, on the reader's own repo. No fixture ships because there is no shareable input.
Timings are self-reported PowerShell Measure-Command values over a handful of runs, not a measured distribution. Treat the ratio, not the exact seconds, as the signal.
